pi_items.source: https://cameochemicals.noaa.gov/chemical/18011 - Fire Hazards section states: 'Can react with air fast enough to cause ignition of near-by combustible material if the heat builds up in an unventilated space (called spontaneous combustion in oily rags)'
https://cameochemicals.noaa.gov/chemical/12667 - Fire Hazards section does not mention spontaneous combustion. Autoignition temperature is 488°F
https://cameochemicals.noaa.gov/chemical/12667 - Classified as hydrocarbon fuel, reacts with oxidizers but is not an oxidizer
https://cameochemicals.noaa.gov/chemical/8 - Fire Hazards do not mention spontaneous combustion. NFPA shows stability rating of 0
https://cameochemicals.noaa.gov/chemical/8 - Classified as flammable liquid with NFPA instability/reactivity of 0
https://cameochemicals.noaa.gov/chemical/8871 - Normally stable even under fire conditions. Autoignition temperature is 540°F
https://cameochemicals.noaa.gov/chemical/8871 - Classified as hydrocarbon, incompatible with oxidizers but not an oxidizer itself

https://cameochemicals.noaa.gov/chemical/890 - Fire Hazards section does not mention spontaneous combustion of the chemical itself. Decomposition can ignite nearby combustibles when contacting certain materials, but this is not inherent spontaneous combustion
https://cameochemicals.noaa.gov/chemical/890 - Classified as 5.1 Oxidizer with NFPA OX designation and noted as 'Strong Oxidizing Agent'

privileged_info_summary: The CAMEO Chemicals database confirms all metadata claims. Linseed oil has a flash point of 432°F and presents genuine spontaneous combustion risk in oily rags/waste. Turpentine has a flash point of 95°F with no spontaneous combustion risk. Acetone has a flash point of 0°F with no spontaneous combustion risk. Stoddard solvent has a flash point of 110°F with no spontaneous combustion risk. Hydrogen peroxide (20-60% concentration) has no reportable flash point and is not spontaneously combustible on its own, but is a strong oxidizing agent. All five chemicals and their properties are real, verifiable data sourced directly from NOAA's CAMEO Chemicals database.
